name: Codec

unions.name: Codec
unions.comment: A value that knows how to encode and decode JSON values.

unions.name: CustomCodec
unions.comment: A partially built `Codec` for a custom type.

unions.name: ObjectCodec
unions.comment: A partially built `Codec` for an object.

aliases.name: Decoder
aliases.comment: A value that knows how to decode JSON values.

aliases.name: Error
aliases.comment:
A structured error describing exactly how the decoder failed. You can use
this to create more elaborate visualizations of a decoder problem. For example,
you could show the entire JSON object and show the part causing the failure in
red.

values.name: andThen
values.comment: Create codecs that depend on previous results.
values.type: (a -> Codec.Codec b) -> (b -> a) -> Codec.Codec a -> Codec.Codec b

values.name: array
values.comment: `Codec` between a JSON array and an Elm `Array`.

values.name: bool
values.comment: `Codec` between a JSON boolean and an Elm `Bool`

values.name: build
values.comment:
Build your own custom `Codec`.
Useful if you have pre-existing `Decoder`s you need to use.

values.name: buildCustom
values.comment: Build a `Codec` for a fully specified custom type.

values.name: buildObject
values.comment: Create a `Codec` from a fully specified `ObjectCodec`.

values.name: char
values.comment: `Codec` between a JSON string of length 1 and an Elm `Char`

values.name: constant
values.comment: Create a `Codec` that produces null as JSON and always decodes as the same value. Obsolete alias of `succeed`, will be removed in a future version.

values.name: custom
values.comment:
Starts building a `Codec` for a custom type.

You need to pass a pattern matching function, built like this:

    type Semaphore
        = Red Int String
        | Yellow
        | Green Float

    semaphoreCodec : Codec Semaphore
    semaphoreCodec =
        Codec.custom
            (\red yellow green value ->
                case value of
                    Red i s ->
                        red i s

                    Yellow ->
                        yellow

                    Green f ->
                        green f
            )
            |> Codec.variant2 "Red" Red Codec.int Codec.string
            |> Codec.variant0 "Yellow" Yellow
            |> Codec.variant1 "Green" Green Codec.float
            |> Codec.buildCustom

The values are encoded as an object with a `tag` field containing the name you specified and an `args` field containing the list of encoded arguments.

If you need more control over the shape you can use `Codec.Advanced.custom`.

values.name: decodeString
values.comment:
Parse the given string into a JSON value and then run the `Codec` on it.
This will fail if the string is not well-formed JSON or if the `Codec`
fails for some reason.
values.type: Codec.Codec a -> String.String -> Result.Result Codec.Error a

values.name: decodeValue
values.comment:
Run a `Codec` to decode some JSON `Value`. You can send these JSON values
through ports, so that is probably the main time you would use this function.
values.type: Codec.Codec a -> Codec.Value -> Result.Result Codec.Error a

values.name: decoder
values.comment: Extracts the `Decoder` contained inside the `Codec`.

values.name: dict
values.comment: `Codec` between a JSON object and an Elm `Dict`.

values.name: encodeToString
values.comment:
Convert a value into a prettified JSON string. The first argument specifies
the amount of indentation in the result string.

values.name: encodeToValue
values.comment: Convert a value into a Javascript `Value`.

values.name: encoder
values.comment: Extracts the encoding function contained inside the `Codec`.

values.name: enum
values.comment:
`Codec` for a fixed list of Elm values.

This can be used for custom types, if they are really simple:

    type Semaphore = Red | Yellow | Green

    semaphoreCodec : Codec Semaphore
    semaphoreCodec =
        enum Codec.string
            [ ("Red", Red)
            , ("Yellow", Yellow)
            , ("Green", Green)
            ]

    encodeToString 0 semaphoreCodec Red
    --> "\"Red\""
    decodeString semaphoreCodec "\"Red\""
    --> Ok Red

    type Count = One | Two | Three

    countCodec : Codec Count
    countCodec =
        enum Codec.int
            [ (1, One)
            , (2, Two)
            , (3, Three)
            ]

    encodeToString 0 countCodec Two
    --> "2"
    decodeString countCodec "2"
    --> Ok Two

    incompleteCodec : Codec Count
    incompleteCodec =
        enum Codec.int
            [ (1, One)
            , (2, Two)
            ]

    encodeToString 0 incompleteCodec Three
    --> "null"

    decodeString incompleteCodec "3" |> Result.mapError (\_ -> "...")
    --> Err "..."
values.type: Codec.Codec a -> List.List ( a, b ) -> Codec.Codec b

values.name: fail
values.comment:
Ignore the JSON and make the decoder fail. This is handy when used with
`oneOf` or `andThen` where you want to give a custom error message in some
case. The encoder will produce `null`.

values.name: field
values.comment:
Specify the name, getter and `Codec` for a field.

The name is only used as the field name in the resulting JSON, and has no impact on the Elm side.
values.type: String.String -> (a -> f) -> Codec.Codec f -> Codec.ObjectCodec a (f -> b) -> Codec.ObjectCodec a b

values.name: float
values.comment: `Codec` between a JSON number and an Elm `Float`

values.name: getFields
values.comment: Get all of the fields present in an ObjectCodec. This might be useful if you are making a request to a database and you want to both decode the response and be able to include in the request a filter so you only receive the fields you intend to decode.

values.name: int
values.comment: `Codec` between a JSON number and an Elm `Int`

values.name: lazy
values.comment:
This is useful for recursive structures that are not easily modeled with `recursive`.
Have a look at the Json.Decode docs for examples.

values.name: list
values.comment: `Codec` between a JSON array and an Elm `List`.

values.name: map
values.comment: Transform a `Codec`.
values.type: (a -> b) -> (b -> a) -> Codec.Codec a -> Codec.Codec b

values.name: maybe
values.comment:
Represents an optional value.

This is encoded as `null` when the input is `Nothing`, and the same as `x` when `Just x`.

If the decoding using the inner `Codec` fails, it will _succeed_ with `Nothing`. If you want it to fail use `nullable` instead.

    encodeToString 0 (maybe int) (Just 3)
    --> "3"
    encodeToString 0 (maybe int) Nothing
    --> "null"

    decodeString (maybe int) "3"
    --> Ok (Just 3)
    decodeString (maybe int) "null"
    --> Ok Nothing
    decodeString (maybe int) "\"hello\""
    --> Ok Nothing

values.name: maybeField
values.comment:
Specify the name getter and `Codec` for an optional field.

**Warning! This is a footgun and thus deprecated, you should probably use `optionalField` instead.**

This is particularly useful for evolving your `Codec`s.

If the field is not present in the input then it gets decoded to `Nothing`. \_If the field value cannot be decoded by the given `Codec` it also gets decoded to `Nothing`. Even worse, if the input is not an object, this `Codec` still succeeds!

When encoding, if the optional field's value is `Nothing` then the resulting object will not contain that field.
values.type: String.String -> (a -> Maybe.Maybe f) -> Codec.Codec f -> Codec.ObjectCodec a (Maybe.Maybe f -> b) -> Codec.ObjectCodec a b

values.name: nullable
values.comment:
Represents an optional value.

This is encoded as `null` when the input is `Nothing`, and the same as `x` when `Just x`.

When decoding, it decodes `null` to `Nothing`. Otherwise, if the decoding using the inner `Codec` fails, it will fail. If you want it to succeed with `Nothing` use `maybe` instead.

    encodeToString 0 (nullable int) (Just 3)
    --> "3"
    encodeToString 0 (nullable int) Nothing
    --> "null"

    decodeString (nullable int) "3"
    --> Ok (Just 3)
    decodeString (nullable int) "null"
    --> Ok Nothing
    decodeString (nullable int) "\"hello\"" |> Result.mapError (\_ -> "...")
    --> Err "..."

values.name: nullableField
values.comment:
Specify the name getter and `Codec` for a required field, whose value can be `null`.

**Warning! This is a footgun and thus deprecated, you should probably use `field` with `nullable` instead.**

If the field is not present in the input then _the decoding fails_. If the field is present but can't be decoded then _the decoding succeeds with `Nothing`_.
If the field's value is `Nothing` then the resulting object will contain the field with a `null` value.

This is a shorthand for a field having a `Codec` built using `maybe`.
values.type: String.String -> (a -> Maybe.Maybe f) -> Codec.Codec f -> Codec.ObjectCodec a (Maybe.Maybe f -> b) -> Codec.ObjectCodec a b

values.name: object
values.comment:
Start creating a `Codec` for an object. You should pass the main constructor as argument.
If you don't have one (for example it's a simple type with no name), you should pass a function that given the field values builds an object.

Example with constructor:

    type alias Point =
        { x : Float
        , y : Float
        }

    pointCodec : Codec Point
    pointCodec =
        Codec.object Point
            |> Codec.field "x" .x Codec.float
            |> Codec.field "y" .y Codec.float
            |> Codec.buildObject

Example without constructor:

    pointCodec : Codec { x : Int, y : Bool }
    pointCodec =
        Codec.object (\x y -> { x = x, y = y })
            |> Codec.field "x" .x Codec.int
            |> Codec.field "y" .y Codec.bool
            |> Codec.buildObject

values.name: oneOf
values.comment:
Try a set of decoders (in order).
The first argument is used for encoding and decoding, the list of other codecs is used as a fallback while decoding.

This is particularly useful for backwards compatibility. You would pass the current codec as the first argument,
and the old ones (eventually `map`ped) as a fallback list to use while decoding.
values.type: Codec.Codec a -> List.List (Codec.Codec a) -> Codec.Codec a

values.name: optionalField
values.comment:
Specify the name getter and `Codec` for an optional field.

This is particularly useful for evolving your `Codec`s.

If the field is not present in the input then it gets decoded to `Nothing`. If the field cannot be decoded this will fail. If the value is `null` then this will fail, use `optionalNullableField` if you want it to succeed instad.
If the optional field's value is `Nothing` then the resulting object will not contain that field.
values.type: String.String -> (a -> Maybe.Maybe f) -> Codec.Codec f -> Codec.ObjectCodec a (Maybe.Maybe f -> b) -> Codec.ObjectCodec a b

values.name: optionalMaybeField
values.comment:
An object field that might not be present or might contain data that maps to Nothing.
This function flattens both into Nothing rather than making you deal with a `Maybe (Maybe a)` type.
When encoding, if the value is Nothing then the field is not included.
values.type: String.String -> (a -> Maybe.Maybe f) -> Codec.Codec (Maybe.Maybe f) -> Codec.ObjectCodec a (Maybe.Maybe f -> b) -> Codec.ObjectCodec a b

values.name: optionalNullableField
values.comment:
Specify the name getter and `Codec` for an optional field.

This is particularly useful for evolving your `Codec`s.

If the field is not present in the input then it gets decoded to `Nothing`. If the field cannot be decoded this will fail. If the value is `null` then this will succeed with `Nothing`, use `optionalField` if you want it to fail instad.
If the optional field's value is `Nothing` then the resulting object will not contain that field.
values.type: String.String -> (a -> Maybe.Maybe f) -> Codec.Codec f -> Codec.ObjectCodec a (Maybe.Maybe f -> b) -> Codec.ObjectCodec a b

values.name: recursive
values.comment:
Create a `Codec` for a recursive data structure.
The argument to the function you need to pass is the fully formed `Codec`.

values.name: result
values.comment: `Codec` for `Result` values.
values.type: Codec.Codec error -> Codec.Codec value -> Codec.Codec (Result.Result error value)

values.name: set
values.comment: `Codec` between a JSON array and an Elm `Set`.

values.name: string
values.comment: `Codec` between a JSON string and an Elm `String`

values.name: succeed
values.comment: Create a `Codec` that produces null as JSON and always decodes as the same value.

values.name: triple
values.comment: `Codec` between a JSON array of length 3 and an Elm triple.
values.type: Codec.Codec a -> Codec.Codec b -> Codec.Codec c -> Codec.Codec ( a, b, c )

values.name: tuple
values.comment: `Codec` between a JSON array of length 2 and an Elm `Tuple`.
values.type: Codec.Codec a -> Codec.Codec b -> Codec.Codec ( a, b )

values.name: value
values.comment: Create a `Codec` that doesn't transform the JSON value, just brings it to and from Elm as a `Value`.

values.name: variant0
values.comment: Define a variant with 0 parameters for a custom type.
values.type: String.String -> v -> Codec.CustomCodec (Codec.Value -> a) v -> Codec.CustomCodec a v

values.name: variant1
values.comment: Define a variant with 1 parameters for a custom type.
values.type: String.String -> (a -> v) -> Codec.Codec a -> Codec.CustomCodec ((a -> Codec.Value) -> b) v -> Codec.CustomCodec b v

values.name: variant2
values.comment: Define a variant with 2 parameters for a custom type.
values.type: String.String -> (a -> b -> v) -> Codec.Codec a -> Codec.Codec b -> Codec.CustomCodec ((a -> b -> Codec.Value) -> c) v -> Codec.CustomCodec c v

values.name: variant3
values.comment: Define a variant with 3 parameters for a custom type.
values.type: String.String -> (a -> b -> c -> v) -> Codec.Codec a -> Codec.Codec b -> Codec.Codec c -> Codec.CustomCodec ((a -> b -> c -> Codec.Value) -> partial) v -> Codec.CustomCodec partial v

values.name: variant4
values.comment: Define a variant with 4 parameters for a custom type.
values.type: String.String -> (a -> b -> c -> d -> v) -> Codec.Codec a -> Codec.Codec b -> Codec.Codec c -> Codec.Codec d -> Codec.CustomCodec ((a -> b -> c -> d -> Codec.Value) -> partial) v -> Codec.CustomCodec partial v

values.name: variant5
values.comment: Define a variant with 5 parameters for a custom type.
values.type: String.String -> (a -> b -> c -> d -> e -> v) -> Codec.Codec a -> Codec.Codec b -> Codec.Codec c -> Codec.Codec d -> Codec.Codec e -> Codec.CustomCodec ((a -> b -> c -> d -> e -> Codec.Value) -> partial) v -> Codec.CustomCodec partial v

values.name: variant6
values.comment: Define a variant with 6 parameters for a custom type.
values.type: String.String -> (a -> b -> c -> d -> e -> f -> v) -> Codec.Codec a -> Codec.Codec b -> Codec.Codec c -> Codec.Codec d -> Codec.Codec e -> Codec.Codec f -> Codec.CustomCodec ((a -> b -> c -> d -> e -> f -> Codec.Value) -> partial) v -> Codec.CustomCodec partial v

values.name: variant7
values.comment: Define a variant with 7 parameters for a custom type.
values.type: String.String -> (a -> b -> c -> d -> e -> f -> g -> v) -> Codec.Codec a -> Codec.Codec b -> Codec.Codec c -> Codec.Codec d -> Codec.Codec e -> Codec.Codec f -> Codec.Codec g -> Codec.CustomCodec ((a -> b -> c -> d -> e -> f -> g -> Codec.Value) -> partial) v -> Codec.CustomCodec partial v

values.name: variant8
values.comment: Define a variant with 8 parameters for a custom type.
values.type: String.String -> (a -> b -> c -> d -> e -> f -> g -> h -> v) -> Codec.Codec a -> Codec.Codec b -> Codec.Codec c -> Codec.Codec d -> Codec.Codec e -> Codec.Codec f -> Codec.Codec g -> Codec.Codec h -> Codec.CustomCodec ((a -> b -> c -> d -> e -> f -> g -> h -> Codec.Value) -> partial) v -> Codec.CustomCodec partial v

values.name: build
values.comment: Build a `Codec` from a fully build `AdvancedCodec`.

values.name: custom
values.comment:
Starts building an advanced `Codec`, usually used for a custom type.

This version allows you to control how variants are encoded, but will use `Json.Decode.oneOf` under the hood, so if two variants have the same shape when encoded, it will always use the first one for decoding.

**⚠️ In particular, in the example below swapping the `Green` and `Yellow` variants means that `Green` values will be decoded as `Yellow` (because `Codec.succeed` always succeeds). ⚠️**

    type Semaphore
        = Red Int String
        | Yellow
        | Green Float

    semaphoreCodec : Codec Semaphore
    semaphoreCodec =
        Codec.Advanced.custom
            (\red green yellow value ->
                case value of
                    Red i s ->
                        red ( i, s )

                    Yellow ->
                        yellow ()

                    Green f ->
                        green f
            )
            |> Codec.Advanced.variant
                (\( i, s ) -> Red i s)
                (Codec.tuple Codec.int Codec.string)
            |> Codec.Advanced.variant
                Green
                Codec.float
            |> Codec.Advanced.variant
                (\() -> Yellow)
                (Codec.succeed ())
            |> Codec.Advanced.build

values.name: variant
values.comment:
Define a variant for an advanced codec.

The first argument is a function from the encoded type to your custom type, the second argument is a codec for the encoded type.
values.type: (a -> v) -> Codec.Codec a -> Codec.Advanced.AdvancedCodec ((a -> Codec.Value) -> b) v -> Codec.Advanced.AdvancedCodec b v
